I respect that he’s successful in tech, but I don’t really trust him as a long-term financial forecaster—he has every reason to talk up Bitcoin.  
  3. Would I look him up?  Probably not beyond maybe glancing at a Wikipedia blurb.  If I did, I’d see he’s heavily invested in Bitcoin infrastructure, so I’d figure his half-million forecast is self-serving hype.  

2) Cathie Wood—$500,000  
  1. Do I know her?  No.  
  2. N/A  
  3. Would I look her up?  Unlikely—I don’t follow stock-market gurus.  If I ever stumbled across her, I’d learn she runs ARK Invest and loves high-flying tech.  That would make me even more skeptical—she’s built a reputation on big, optimistic bets, not proven steady returns.  

3) Peter Schiff—$3,000  
  1. Do I know him?  Yes.  
  2. What I know and trust level:  He’s the gold-bug fellow on Fox Business and conservative talk radio, always warning about Fed money printing.  He’s been calling for gold to go way up for years and dismisses Bitcoin as a fad.  I respect that he speaks truth to power on inflation, but he’s biased toward gold, so I take his ultra-bearish Bitcoin take with a grain of salt.  

4) Jamie Dimon—$5,000  
  1. Do I know him?  Vaguely.  
  2. What I know and trust level:  He’s the head of JPMorgan Chase, one of the biggest banks in the world.  Banks generally hate Bitcoin because it threatens them.  I don’t trust his low-ball number—it’s in his bank’s interest to downplay crypto.  
  3. Would I look him up?  No need—I see his vested interest every time I watch the evening news.  

Bottom line: I’d dismiss the ultra-bullish $500K calls as coming from people who stand to gain if Bitcoin soars, and I’d discount the low forecasts from those who see Bitcoin as a threat. I’d probably boil it down to “people are split—and I’m not about to stake my retirement on a crypto coin.”

   Probably not in depth—I’d skim a news headline to remind myself who he is. If I did, I’d find that his businesses earn money by building crypto tools, so he has a vested interest in pumping Bitcoin’s prospects. That would make me more cautious about accepting $500,000 as realistic.
type_b_raw:
PREDICTION: 50000  
REASONING: I respect Buffett’s cautious view but believe Bitcoin has practical demand. I discount Jamie Dimon’s low forecast as bank self-interest and Dorsey’s high number as tech hype. Trump’s number feels driven by politics more than market fundamentals. On balance I see moderate adoption lifting price to around fifty thousand.
